cards.question: What does Charlie Munger mean when he says you need 'models in your head' arranged as a 'latticework'?
options: One discipline holds most of the world's real wisdom
Isolated facts are not usable unless they hang on a framework of theory
Real-world experience replaces the need for theoretical models
Memorizing enough facts eventually makes expertise automatic
cards.correct_answer: Isolated facts are not usable unless they hang on a framework of theory
cards.explanation: The quoted Munger passage says isolated facts you try to 'bang back' are useless unless they 'hang together on a latticework of theory.' He also directly rejects the single-discipline view ('all the wisdom of the world is not to be found in one little academic department'), which is why the one-discipline distractor is wrong.

cards.question: What distinguishes a 'super model' from an ordinary mental model in the authors' framework?
options: It produces mathematically precise predictions
It was first articulated by Charlie Munger himself
It transfers usefully beyond the discipline it originated in
It replaces the need for domain-specific expertise
cards.correct_answer: It transfers usefully beyond the discipline it originated in
cards.explanation: The text contrasts critical mass (broadly useful outside physics) with 'hundreds of other concepts from physics' that stay in their discipline — super models are specifically the ones with metaphorical value well beyond their origin. Munger introduced the authors to the concept; he did not invent super models.

cards.question: The authors compare using mental models to which arithmetic operation, and why?
options: Exponentiation versus addition, because models compound over time
Division versus subtraction, because models split problems into parts
Multiplication versus addition, because models let you skip tedious lower-level reasoning
Algebra versus arithmetic, because models introduce variables
cards.correct_answer: Multiplication versus addition, because models let you skip tedious lower-level reasoning
cards.explanation: The text says strategic thinking without mental models 'is like using addition when multiplication is available' — 7 added to itself 158 times versus 7 × 158. Without models you start from scratch every time instead of jumping to higher-level reasoning.

cards.question: What does Carl Jacobi's maxim 'Invert, always invert' mean in the book's framing?
options: Always do the opposite of your initial instinct
Approaching a problem from its inverse perspective can unlock new strategies
Reverse the order of steps in any decision process
Argue the opposite of whatever your opponent claims
cards.correct_answer: Approaching a problem from its inverse perspective can unlock new strategies
cards.explanation: The text gives concrete examples — investing to not lose money (inverse of making money) and avoiding unhealthy options (inverse of constructing a healthy diet). The principle is flipping the perspective on the problem, not reversing sequences, instincts, or arguments.

cards.question: In the book's usage, an 'unforced error' is...
options: A mistake caused by circumstances outside your control
A tactical concession made to win a larger strategic gain
An avoidable mistake caused by your own poor judgment, not an opponent's skill
A decision that was right given the information but turned out badly
cards.correct_answer: An avoidable mistake caused by your own poor judgment, not an opponent's skill
cards.explanation: The tennis definition — hitting an easy ball into the net — grounds the metaphor: unforced errors are self-inflicted and avoidable. The text explicitly distinguishes this from 'the best decision based on the information available at the time' that turns out wrong due to uncertainty.

cards.question: According to Taleb as quoted in the text, what distinguishes 'antifragile' from merely 'resilient'?
options: Antifragile things get better when exposed to shocks; resilient ones just stay the same
Antifragile things are immune to shocks; resilient ones recover slowly
Antifragile things avoid shocks entirely; resilient ones endure them
Antifragile things are predictable; resilient ones are merely rigid
cards.correct_answer: Antifragile things get better when exposed to shocks; resilient ones just stay the same
cards.explanation: Taleb's quoted line is explicit: 'The resilient resists shocks and stays the same; the antifragile gets better.' Immunity and avoidance misstate the contrast — antifragility is not about absence of shocks but about benefiting from them.

cards.question: What does 'arguing from first principles' mean?
options: Following the most widely accepted explanation in a field
Starting from the conclusions experts have already reached
Reasoning up from basic, self-evident assumptions, bypassing conventional wisdom
Relying on analogies to familiar situations
cards.correct_answer: Reasoning up from basic, self-evident assumptions, bypassing conventional wisdom
cards.explanation: The text defines it as 'thinking from the bottom up, using basic building blocks of what you think is true.' Musk's battery example — computing raw material costs rather than accepting the prevailing $600/kWh figure — shows this in action. Conventional wisdom is explicitly what first-principles thinking avoids.

cards.question: Ockham's razor advises that when competing explanations fit the data equally well, you should...
options: Pick the most recently proposed explanation
Choose the simplest one to investigate first
Choose the one that matches expert consensus
Combine all of them into a single hybrid theory
cards.correct_answer: Choose the simplest one to investigate first
cards.explanation: The text says Ockham's razor 'shaves off unnecessary assumptions' and advises investigating the simplest plausible explanation first — captured by the medical saying 'When you hear hoofbeats, think of horses, not zebras.' It is not a law of truth, just a starting-point heuristic.

cards.section: Chapter 2 — Anything That Can Go Wrong, Will
cards.question: The 'tragedy of the commons' arises when...
options: A single bad actor deliberately destroys a communal asset
A private resource is taxed into unprofitability
A public good is protected by regulation that prevents its use
Individually rational uses of a shared resource collectively deplete it
cards.correct_answer: Individually rational uses of a shared resource collectively deplete it
cards.explanation: The Boston Common overgrazing story grounds the model: each farmer rationally benefits from one more cow, but collectively the commons is depleted. It is a collective failure of individually rational choices, not single-actor sabotage, over-taxation, or over-regulation.

cards.section: Chapter 2 — Anything That Can Go Wrong, Will
cards.question: What is the 'tyranny of small decisions' as the text defines it?
options: Individually rational small decisions aggregating into a system-wide negative consequence
A dictator imposing many minor rules that together feel oppressive
One large decision being unfairly treated as unimportant
Teams fragmenting a big decision into too many sub-decisions for efficiency
cards.correct_answer: Individually rational small decisions aggregating into a system-wide negative consequence
cards.explanation: The text quotes ecologist Odum on environmental problems 'resulting from a series of small decisions,' and gives the split-check dinner and small-credit-card-purchases examples. The other options describe political tyranny, miscategorization, or decomposition — none of which is what the model names.

cards.section: Chapter 2 — Anything That Can Go Wrong, Will
cards.question: In the vaccination example, a person who skips vaccination but benefits from herd immunity is exhibiting which model?
options: Survivorship bias — surviving because others took the risk
Free rider problem — using a public good without bearing its cost
Selection bias — self-selecting into the immune group
Observer-expectancy effect — expecting not to catch the disease
cards.correct_answer: Free rider problem — using a public good without bearing its cost
cards.explanation: The text explicitly names it: 'the free riders are those who take advantage of this public good by not getting vaccinated.' The bias options all belong to research methodology (Chapter 5) and do not describe the commons/public-good dynamic at issue.

cards.section: Chapter 2 — Anything That Can Go Wrong, Will
cards.question: An 'externality' in the book's economic sense is...
options: A cost deliberately shifted onto a buyer through pricing
A benefit shared equally by all participants in a market
A consequence of an action that affects an entity without its consent
An unexpected outcome that only affects the decision-maker
cards.correct_answer: A consequence of an action that affects an entity without its consent
cards.explanation: The text defines externalities as 'consequences, good or bad, that affect an entity without its consent, imposed from an external source' — the vaccine (positive) and factory air pollution (negative) examples ground it. Deliberate pricing transfers and participant-only outcomes are not the externality concept.

cards.question: In the book's usage, a 'north star' is...
options: A daily to-do list ordered by urgency
A specific, measurable one-year goal
A mentor whose career path you imitate
The guiding long-term vision toward which you orient your actions
cards.correct_answer: The guiding long-term vision toward which you orient your actions
cards.explanation: The text builds the metaphor from Polaris as a fixed navigational reference, then defines a north star as 'the guiding vision of a company' or a personal mission statement — explicitly long-term orientation. A day-list, a short-term goal, or a role model would not serve that navigational function.

cards.question: Why is multitasking on high-concentration activities problematic, according to the text?
options: Your brain cannot focus on two high-concentration activities at once, forcing costly context-switching
It always leads to worse performance regardless of the activities
It trains your brain to produce only shallow work
It is physically harmful to your brain over time
cards.correct_answer: Your brain cannot focus on two high-concentration activities at once, forcing costly context-switching
cards.explanation: The text distinguishes low-concentration (autopilot) and high-concentration (deliberate) thinking, and says only one high-concentration activity can run at a time — context-switching wastes time and mental overhead. The 'always worse' option overreaches: the text explicitly allows low-consequence multitasking like folding laundry while watching TV.

cards.question: In Keith Rabois's story about Peter Thiel at PayPal, why did Thiel insist each person work on only one thing?
options: To make employees easier to manage and evaluate
To minimize coordination overhead between teams
To force people onto high-impact A+ problems they would otherwise procrastinate
To reward specialists over generalists
cards.correct_answer: To force people onto high-impact A+ problems they would otherwise procrastinate
cards.explanation: Rabois's quote explains that most people default to solvable B+ problems, leaving high-impact A+ problems untouched — Thiel's one-thing rule prevents that escape hatch. Management simplicity, coordination overhead, and specialist rewards are not reasons the text gives.

cards.question: In the Eisenhower Decision Matrix, activities in quadrant II (Not Urgent / Important) should be...
options: Handled immediately before anything else
Delegated, outsourced, or ignored
Prioritized and focused on, since this is where deep work and long-term progress happen
Reduced or eliminated altogether
cards.correct_answer: Prioritized and focused on, since this is where deep work and long-term progress happen
cards.explanation: The text labels quadrant II 'FOCUS' (strategic planning, deep work, relationship-building) and says these activities should get your creative energy, ranked right after true emergencies. Delegation is the advice for quadrant III; reduction is for quadrant IV.

cards.question: The peppered moth's rise from 0.01% to 98% dark-colored in Manchester illustrates which mental model?
options: Inertia — dark moths resisted change once established
The Lindy effect — older traits outlast newer ones
Natural selection — traits offering reproductive advantage propagate as the environment changes
The Shirky principle — institutions preserving the problems they solve
cards.correct_answer: Natural selection — traits offering reproductive advantage propagate as the environment changes
cards.explanation: Soot-darkened trees flipped the camouflage advantage from light to dark; birds picked off the light moths, so dark genes propagated. The text explicitly calls this 'a showcase of natural selection.' Inertia and Lindy describe persistence, not selection-pressure shifts.

cards.question: What is a 'strategy tax' in the book's usage?
options: A government levy imposed on dominant-market firms
A suboptimal decision an organization accepts because it must stay consistent with its long-term strategy
The cost of adapting strategy to match competitors
The opportunity cost of choosing one strategy over another
cards.correct_answer: A suboptimal decision an organization accepts because it must stay consistent with its long-term strategy
cards.explanation: The Google Chrome example grounds it: Google won't add strong anti-tracking features because its strategy is to dominate ad tracking — a self-imposed strategic constraint, not a government levy or generic adaptation cost.

cards.question: The Shirky principle states that...
options: Systems naturally simplify over time as users demand ease
Institutions will try to preserve the problem to which they are the solution
Organizations expand to fill the resources available to them
Legacy technology is displaced whenever a better alternative appears
cards.correct_answer: Institutions will try to preserve the problem to which they are the solution
cards.explanation: The TurboTax example grounds it: the company lobbies against return-free filing because its business depends on tax filing remaining complex. The simplification, Parkinson's-law, and clean-disruption options describe different (and largely opposite) dynamics.

cards.question: What does the Lindy effect predict about nonperishable things that have already endured for a long time?
options: They are overdue to fail and should be expected to decline soon
They will age and degrade at a fixed rate from now on
Their expected remaining lifespan grows with the time they have already lasted
They have reached peak popularity and will never be more relevant
cards.correct_answer: Their expected remaining lifespan grows with the time they have already lasted
cards.explanation: The text quotes Taleb: a book in print 40 years can be expected another 40; if it survives another decade, expect 50 more — 'the robustness of an item is proportional to its life.' The failure-expected and fixed-decline options invert the effect.

cards.section: Chapter 5 — Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics
cards.question: 'Correlation does not imply causation' means that...
options: Two correlated events must be independent of each other
Causation can never be demonstrated, only correlation
Correlation is always a weaker relationship than a direct causal link
Two events occurring together does not prove one caused the other
cards.correct_answer: Two events occurring together does not prove one caused the other
cards.explanation: The flu-vaccine example grounds it: people get vaccinated during cold-and-flu season, so timing correlation does not mean vaccination caused symptoms. The text never claims correlated events are independent or that causation is unknowable — just that correlation alone does not prove it.

cards.section: Chapter 5 — Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics
cards.question: A 'confounding factor' in a study is...
options: A third variable that influences both the assumed cause and the observed effect
A flaw in data collection that biases the results
An outlier that skews the statistical summary
A participant who fails to follow the experimental protocol
cards.correct_answer: A third variable that influences both the assumed cause and the observed effect
cards.explanation: The text defines a confounder as 'a third, possibly non-obvious factor that influences both the assumed cause and the observed effect' — cold-and-flu season confounds the vaccine/symptoms relationship. The other options describe different methodological issues (bias, outliers, noncompliance).

cards.section: Chapter 5 — Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics
cards.question: Survivorship bias in the WWII aircraft-damage study showed up as...
options: Researchers overweighting the most dramatic damage patterns they observed
Pilots self-selecting into safer missions, biasing the sample
Conclusions drawn only from planes that returned, ignoring those shot down
Older planes surviving longer than newer ones, distorting comparisons
cards.correct_answer: Conclusions drawn only from planes that returned, ignoring those shot down
cards.explanation: Wald's insight was that holes in returning planes marked survivable hit locations; the lethal spots were underrepresented because those planes didn't come back. The bias is in the sampled population — survivors only — not in the weighting of dramatic damage or in mission selection.

cards.section: Chapter 5 — Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics
cards.question: The 'gambler's fallacy' refers to the false belief that...
options: Random sequences contain more streaks than people expect
A streak of one outcome makes the opposite outcome more likely on the next independent trial
The house always wins in the long run
Small samples reliably estimate long-run averages
cards.correct_answer: A streak of one outcome makes the opposite outcome more likely on the next independent trial
cards.explanation: The roulette example grounds it: after ten blacks in a row, the underlying probability of each spin has not changed, but people expect red. The 'streaks are common' statement is actually the clustering illusion — the opposite error: correctly understanding randomness means expecting streaks, not expecting them to self-correct.

cards.question: Which shortcoming of the pro-con list does the chapter emphasize?
options: It requires mathematical training to use effectively
It ignores the decision-maker's emotions
It treats all pros and cons as equally weighted and independent of each other
It takes too long for most real-world decisions
cards.correct_answer: It treats all pros and cons as equally weighted and independent of each other
cards.explanation: The text lists four problems with the pro-con list: only two options presumed, equal-weight treatment, independence treatment, and the grass-is-greener disparity. Training difficulty, emotional blindness, and speed are not among the issues the book raises — the text's complaint is that the method is too simple.

cards.question: A 'cost-benefit analysis' improves on a pro-con list primarily because it...
options: Rules out any option whose costs or benefits are intangible
Assigns quantitative (often dollar) values so options can be compared systematically
Replaces subjective judgment with objective market data
Guarantees the highest-scoring option will succeed
cards.correct_answer: Assigns quantitative (often dollar) values so options can be compared systematically
cards.explanation: The method starts with −10-to-10 scoring, then escalates to explicit dollar values so options can be summed and compared. The text explicitly says intangibles should still be assigned rough dollar values (not ruled out), and never claims the method is purely objective or outcome-guaranteed.

cards.question: In time-based cost-benefit analysis, why is a future benefit worth less than the same benefit today?
options: Taxes are usually higher on future income
Money physically loses value in storage over time
Future benefits are generally taxed twice
Opportunity cost of capital, inflation, and future uncertainty all reduce its present value
cards.correct_answer: Opportunity cost of capital, inflation, and future uncertainty all reduce its present value
cards.explanation: The text gives three explicit reasons: (1) money today can be invested for returns (opportunity cost), (2) inflation reduces future purchasing power, (3) future predictions are uncertain. Tax-based explanations are not reasons the text gives.

cards.question: 'Maslow's hammer' warns that...
options: Having only one tool leads you to treat every problem as if it needs that tool
Using the wrong tool always produces worse results than using no tool
The best tool for any job is usually the most specialized one
Experienced practitioners should avoid learning new tools
cards.correct_answer: Having only one tool leads you to treat every problem as if it needs that tool
cards.explanation: Maslow's quoted car-washing-machine passage grounds it: a tool with only one function treats everything as its target. The issue is an overly narrow toolkit, not that wrong tools are worse than no tools, nor about specialization or learning avoidance.

cards.question: An 'arms race' in the book's general usage describes...
options: A legally sanctioned buildup of defensive capabilities
A negotiation where both sides steadily concede less
Any escalating competition where all sides continually expend resources to keep up
A contest with a predetermined endpoint and winner
cards.correct_answer: Any escalating competition where all sides continually expend resources to keep up
cards.explanation: The text extends the term beyond literal weapons to luxury campus amenities, credential inflation, and 'keeping up with the Joneses.' The defining feature is escalation with no clear end, not legal sanction, concession, or a preset endpoint.

cards.question: In the prisoner's dilemma as laid out in the text, the Nash equilibrium is...
options: Both prisoners remaining silent (1 year each)
Both prisoners betraying each other (5 years each)
One prisoner betraying, one staying silent (0 vs. 10 years)
Any random mix of strategies across repeated plays
cards.correct_answer: Both prisoners betraying each other (5 years each)
cards.explanation: The text defines Nash equilibrium as a strategy set where no single player can improve by unilaterally switching. Dual betrayal satisfies this: from 5/5, switching to silence yields 10 years. Dual silence is strictly better in total but unstable — either player can improve by defecting, so it is not an equilibrium.

cards.question: Why does a 'tit-for-tat' strategy tend to outperform constant betrayal in an iterated prisoner's dilemma?
options: It guarantees the highest score in any single round
It enables cooperation when opponents cooperate while still punishing defection
It prevents opponents from ever defecting in the first place
It maximizes short-term individual gain each round
cards.correct_answer: It enables cooperation when opponents cooperate while still punishing defection
cards.explanation: The text says tit-for-tat starts cooperatively and then follows the opponent's last move, enabling sustained cooperation with a credible retaliation threat. It is a long-term/reputation strategy, not a single-round maximizer, and it cannot prevent an opponent from defecting — only respond to it.

cards.question: Why does the text argue that 'iterated games' change cooperative outcomes compared to single-shot games?
options: Random starting conditions each round dilute strategic commitment
Simultaneous moves eliminate the advantage of going first
Repeated interaction makes reputation and future retribution meaningful
Zero-sum endings force one side to lose everything eventually
cards.correct_answer: Repeated interaction makes reputation and future retribution meaningful
cards.explanation: The text contrasts single-shot and iterated play, saying repetition introduces reputation and the possibility of future retribution, which is what makes cooperation sustainable. The other options describe unrelated game properties (randomization, simultaneity, zero-sum endings) that aren't what distinguishes iterated from one-shot play.

cards.question: 'Joy's law,' as stated by Bill Joy, says that...
options: No matter who you are, most of the smartest people work for someone else
Talent is distributed evenly across organizations of similar size
The smartest person in the room should always lead it
Teams outperform individuals on almost every complex task
cards.correct_answer: No matter who you are, most of the smartest people work for someone else
cards.explanation: The text quotes Joy directly. The 'even distribution' option contradicts the quote; the leadership and teams-vs-individuals options address unrelated management questions. The law's specific point is about where top talent is concentrated relative to any single organization.

cards.question: The '10x engineer' concept, as applied cautiously in the text, suggests...
options: Truly talented individuals produce 10x output regardless of context
Outsized output often depends on role, team, and resources — not only innate skill
Every organization should aim to hire only 10x engineers
10x output can only come from formally credentialed experts
cards.correct_answer: Outsized output often depends on role, team, and resources — not only innate skill
cards.explanation: The text names two traps: 10x people are rare, and — more subtly — outsized output often reflects role, personality fit, resources, and relationships, and may not transfer when those change. This directly contradicts the context-independence claim and the hire-only-10x prescription.

cards.question: Why isn't IQ alone sufficient for evaluating someone's fit for a leadership or sales role, according to the text?
options: IQ tests are widely considered culturally biased
Leadership depends mostly on experience, not intelligence
Those roles rely on emotional intelligence (EQ), which is independent of IQ
EQ is a stronger predictor of performance in every role
cards.correct_answer: Those roles rely on emotional intelligence (EQ), which is independent of IQ
cards.explanation: The text defines EQ as the ability to perceive, manage, and use emotional states, says project management, leadership, sales, and marketing are best suited to high-EQ people, and explicitly notes IQ and EQ are independent traits. The 'EQ stronger in every role' option overgeneralizes past what the text claims.

cards.question: What does the text say about using personality traits like introversion/extroversion in staffing decisions?
options: Personality traits can be reliably changed with the right incentives
The best teams are made up of people with identical personality profiles
Extroversion is the most valuable trait in nearly every role
Traits are durable, so you should shape roles to fit them rather than expect them to change
cards.correct_answer: Traits are durable, so you should shape roles to fit them rather than expect them to change
cards.explanation: The text says most personality traits aren't quick to change once established — 'an introvert isn't likely to become an extrovert' — so you should 'look to accommodate these traits in the roles you select.' The text does not endorse identical profiles and gives examples where extroversion is wrong (e.g., solitary programming).

cards.question: 'Arbitrage,' in the book's sense, is...
options: Investing in companies with sustainable competitive advantage
Buying high and selling higher on the same exchange
Profiting from price differences for the same product across different settings
Creating new value by combining unrelated products
cards.correct_answer: Profiting from price differences for the same product across different settings
cards.explanation: The Hatchimals example ($60 retail, $1,200 on eBay) and Lauren's eBay relisting story both show the same product trading at different prices in different venues. The text explicitly contrasts arbitrage with sustainable competitive advantage — they are opposites in the chapter's framing.

cards.question: 'Market power,' as defined in the text, is...
options: A government-granted monopoly license
The total revenue of the largest firm in a market
The percentage of consumers who recognize a brand
The power to profitably raise prices in a market
cards.correct_answer: The power to profitably raise prices in a market
cards.explanation: The text defines market power as 'the power to profitably raise prices in a market' and cites Amazon Prime hikes that didn't drive customers away. Monopolies are examples of high market power but not the definition; revenue and brand recognition are not the measure the text uses.

cards.question: 'Perfect competition,' according to the text, describes markets where...
options: A single firm dominates and sets prices freely
Many competitors provide identical products (commodities), leaving no supplier with pricing power
Every buyer and seller has complete information
Prices are set by government regulation
cards.correct_answer: Many competitors provide identical products (commodities), leaving no supplier with pricing power
cards.explanation: The text defines perfect competition using isopropyl alcohol as the canonical example: perfect substitutes, so raising prices just loses customers to the next supplier. Single-firm dominance is monopoly (the opposite), and government price-setting is not part of the definition.

cards.question: The consensus-contrarian matrix attributed to Howard Marks says that outsized investment returns require being...
options: Right and consensus
Right and contrarian
Wrong and contrarian
Right more often than wrong
cards.correct_answer: Right and contrarian
cards.explanation: Rachleff's quoted matrix: consensus-right earns 'regular-sized returns'; contrarian-right earns 'outsized returns'; being wrong (either stance) earns no return. 'Right more often than wrong' doesn't address the consensus-vs-contrarian axis the matrix is built on.

cards.question: Feynman's 'cargo cult' metaphor warns against...
options: Borrowing practices from other cultures without giving credit
Following scientific protocols that are too rigid for the situation
Abandoning proven methods for fashionable new ones
Imitating the form of a practice without understanding what actually produces the results
cards.correct_answer: Imitating the form of a practice without understanding what actually produces the results
cards.explanation: The Melanesian villagers built runway-shaped structures hoping planes would come — 'the form is perfect, but it doesn't work.' Applied to thinking, cargo-cult super thinkers use mental models 'without really understanding them.' The other options describe unrelated issues (attribution, rigidity, fashion-chasing).

cards.question: Where does the text say the most dangerous zone for applying mental models lies?
options: Deep inside your circle of competence, where overconfidence builds up
Just outside your circle of competence, where you think you're competent but aren't
Completely outside your circle, where you already know not to act
Any situation where no mental model seems to apply
cards.correct_answer: Just outside your circle of competence, where you think you're competent but aren't
cards.explanation: The text says explicitly: 'The most dangerous zone is just outside your circle of competence, where you might think you are competent but you really are not,' linking this to the Dunning-Kruger effect. Knowing you're outside the circle is actually safer — caution follows awareness.

cards.question: In the book's framing, what does 'de-risking' an assumption mean?
options: Removing the assumption from your plan altogether
Combining the assumption with other assumptions to average out risk
Buying insurance against the assumption being wrong
Testing the assumption in the real world to see whether it holds
cards.correct_answer: Testing the assumption in the real world to see whether it holds
cards.explanation: The text says that to be wrong less, you must test your assumptions 'in the real world, a process known as de-risking.' The vacation example — checking reviews, emailing relatives — grounds this: de-risking is empirical testing, not removal, insurance, or averaging.

cards.question: What does 'premature optimization' describe as a mistake?
options: Optimizing for the wrong metric and missing the real goal
Investing in long-term infrastructure instead of short-term fixes
Launching a product before it has enough features to be useful
Tweaking or perfecting work before testing the underlying assumptions in reality
cards.correct_answer: Tweaking or perfecting work before testing the underlying assumptions in reality
cards.explanation: The text defines it as tweaking or perfecting code or algorithms 'too early (prematurely),' using the vacation example where you book everything before asking whether relatives can come. The wrong-metric and under-featured-launch options describe different problems; the long-term-infrastructure option contradicts the text, which warns against doing too much work before testing.

cards.question: Why do Tversky and Kahneman's 'Linda' bank-teller example illustrate the conjunction fallacy?
options: People wrongly rate the more specific combined description as more probable than the general one
People ignore base rates of feminists and bank tellers in the population
People confuse correlation between feminism and bank-telling with causation
People average two probabilities instead of multiplying them
cards.correct_answer: People wrongly rate the more specific combined description as more probable than the general one
cards.explanation: The text notes most respondents pick 'bank teller AND feminist' as more probable than 'bank teller' — impossible, since the probability of two events together cannot exceed either alone. The base-rate and correlation-causation options describe different fallacies covered elsewhere in the book.

cards.section: In the Eye of the Beholder
cards.question: In Dan Ariely's Economist subscription example, why did adding a $125 print-only option that no one chose still increase overall revenue?
options: It created scarcity pressure on the web-only option
It rewarded loyal print subscribers with an exclusive option
It split demand so more people chose the lower-priced option
It acted as an anchor that made the print-and-web bundle feel like a bargain
cards.correct_answer: It acted as an anchor that made the print-and-web bundle feel like a bargain
cards.explanation: The text says the print-only option 'anchors readers to a much higher value for the print-and-web version' — it feels like you get web free. The text attributes the 43 percent revenue lift entirely to anchoring, not to scarcity, demand splitting, or loyalty dynamics.

cards.section: In the Eye of the Beholder
cards.question: What does the text say about availability bias and media coverage of violent crime?
options: Heavy coverage causes people to overestimate how often violent crime occurs
Coverage lowers crime by deterring would-be offenders
Coverage accurately tracks federal crime statistics year over year
Coverage has no measurable effect on public perception
cards.correct_answer: Heavy coverage causes people to overestimate how often violent crime occurs
cards.explanation: The text cites the 'if it bleeds, it leads' mantra and the Gallup finding that 'federal crime statistics have not been highly relevant to the public's crime perceptions' — coverage drives perception away from the data. The deterrence, accuracy, and no-effect options contradict this.

cards.section: In the Eye of the Beholder
cards.question: According to the text, why do personalized search and social feeds create a 'filter bubble'?
options: Governments require platforms to filter out foreign viewpoints
Search engines use random sampling that happens to match user preferences
Users actively block content they disagree with
Algorithms filter out links you are unlikely to click, including opposing viewpoints, to show you what you already like
cards.correct_answer: Algorithms filter out links you are unlikely to click, including opposing viewpoints, to show you what you already like
cards.explanation: The text says platforms 'filter out links they think you are unlikely to click on, such as opposing viewpoints, effectively placing you in a bubble,' citing DuckDuckGo studies showing personalized political search results even in incognito mode. The government-mandate, user-blocking, and random-sampling options aren't in the text.

cards.section: Walk a Mile in Their Shoes
cards.question: What does the 'most respectful interpretation' (MRI) ask of you?
options: Give up your own point of view to defer to others
Take the middle ground between your view and theirs
Assume the other party is always acting in bad faith until proven otherwise
Interpret the other parties' actions in the most respectful way possible while withholding judgment
cards.correct_answer: Interpret the other parties' actions in the most respectful way possible while withholding judgment
cards.explanation: The text says MRI is giving people the benefit of the doubt — the school example shows this: assuming staff are working on a reply rather than ignoring you. The text explicitly says MRI 'isn't asking you to give up your point of view,' ruling out the defer-entirely and split-the-difference options; assuming bad faith is its opposite.

cards.section: Walk a Mile in Their Shoes
cards.question: Hanlon's razor advises you to...
options: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by carelessness
Always pick the most parsimonious scientific explanation
Assume the other person is smarter than you until you have evidence otherwise
Treat ambiguous behavior as a deliberate signal
cards.correct_answer: Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by carelessness
cards.explanation: The text states Hanlon's razor verbatim and gives the online-message example: a curt 'OK' is more likely a rushed writer than a dismissive one. Parsimony describes Ockham's razor (a different model); the other options aren't what Hanlon's razor says.

cards.section: Walk a Mile in Their Shoes
cards.question: How does the text describe the fundamental attribution error?
options: Attributing others' behavior to their internal character while attributing your own to external circumstances
Confusing first causes with proximate causes
Believing attributes are fixed rather than changeable
Over-weighting recent attributes at the expense of long-term patterns
cards.correct_answer: Attributing others' behavior to their internal character while attributing your own to external circumstances
cards.explanation: The text says you 'make errors by attributing others' behaviors to their internal, or fundamental, motivations rather than external factors,' and pairs this with self-serving bias for your own behavior — the red-light example illustrates both. The other options describe unrelated concepts (cause types, mindsets, recency).

cards.section: Walk a Mile in Their Shoes
cards.question: Why does the text warn that belief in a 'just world' can obstruct justice?
options: It makes people demand overly harsh punishments
It causes courts to ignore documented evidence
It leads people to blame victims for circumstances that actually involve randomness or bad luck
It discourages governments from enforcing laws fairly
cards.correct_answer: It leads people to blame victims for circumstances that actually involve randomness or bad luck
cards.explanation: The text explicitly says belief in a just world 'can get in the way of actual justice by leading people to victim-blame,' with examples like blaming assault victims or welfare recipients, ignoring factors like the birth lottery. The harsh-punishment, evidence, and government-enforcement options are not claims the text makes.

cards.section: Progress, One Funeral at a Time
cards.question: Planck's maxim 'Science progresses one funeral at a time' captures the idea that...
options: New scientific truths convince opponents once they see the evidence
Science advances most when research funding is steady over generations
Scientists should delay publishing until a paradigm shift is fully complete
New theories win mainly because their opponents eventually die and a new generation grows up with them
cards.correct_answer: New theories win mainly because their opponents eventually die and a new generation grows up with them
cards.explanation: The text quotes Planck directly: a new truth 'does not triumph by convincing its opponents and making them see the light, but rather because its opponents eventually die.' The convincing-with-evidence option is exactly what Planck denies; the delay and funding options don't appear in the text.

cards.section: Progress, One Funeral at a Time
cards.question: The 'backfire effect' describes what happens when...
options: People reject weak arguments more readily than strong ones
People change their minds faster when the stakes are higher
Confirmation bias is corrected by exposure to new data
People presented with facts disproving a position dig in further on that position
cards.correct_answer: People presented with facts disproving a position dig in further on that position
cards.explanation: The text defines the backfire effect as 'digging in further on a position when faced with clear evidence that disproves it,' and cites the 2008 Yale John Roberts study where disapproval stayed high even after the ad was refuted. The other options are either the opposite dynamic or not in the text.

cards.section: Progress, One Funeral at a Time
cards.question: What is 'disconfirmation bias' as described in Daniel Gilbert's bathroom-scale analogy?
options: Rejecting evidence you don't like by demanding a higher standard of proof for it
Confirming only evidence that disconfirms your rivals' views
Discounting all evidence that lacks a replicable study behind it
Accepting evidence equally regardless of how it affects your beliefs
cards.correct_answer: Rejecting evidence you don't like by demanding a higher standard of proof for it
cards.explanation: Gilbert's quoted example — hopping off and on the scale only when it delivers bad news — grounds the definition: 'you impose a stronger burden of proof on the ideas you don't want to believe.' The equal-acceptance option is the opposite; the others are not in the text.

cards.question: In the 5 Whys analysis of the Challenger disaster, what did the Rogers Commission identify as the root cause?
options: Hot gas leaking from a solid rocket motor
An O-ring that failed outside its intended temperature range
A lack of proper checks and balances at NASA
Unexpectedly cold weather on launch day
cards.correct_answer: A lack of proper checks and balances at NASA
cards.explanation: The text walks the 5 Whys chain from hydrogen tank ignition down to 'safety concerns were ignored' and finally 'there was a lack of proper checks and balances at NASA. That was the root cause.' The other options are all proximate or intermediate causes in the chain.

cards.question: Why does Feynman's appendix argue NASA management overstated shuttle reliability?
options: Engineers estimated much higher failure probabilities than managers did
NASA's own statistical models predicted certain failure
External auditors independently calculated the correct odds
Astronauts refused to fly on unreliable missions
cards.correct_answer: Engineers estimated much higher failure probabilities than managers did
cards.explanation: Feynman's quoted appendix says failure-probability estimates ranged 'from roughly 1 in 100 to 1 in 100,000,' with high figures coming from working engineers and low ones from management — 'management of NASA exaggerates the reliability of its product, to the point of fantasy.' The other options don't appear in the text.

cards.question: What is 'optimistic probability bias' in the book's usage?
options: Believing optimism itself raises the real probability of success
Weighting recent good outcomes more than earlier bad ones
Preferring optimistic forecasts over pessimistic ones in expert panels
Being too optimistic about the probability of success because you want the outcome badly
cards.correct_answer: Being too optimistic about the probability of success because you want the outcome badly
cards.explanation: The text defines it as fooling yourself into thinking a desired outcome is likely — NASA managers were 'way too optimistic about the probability of success,' whereas engineers closer to the analysis were more accurate. The other options redefine it or describe different biases.

cards.question: What does the text say about 'herd immunity' as a concept beyond the medical context?
options: It only applies when a majority of members are genetically similar
It disappears once social norms are made explicit
It applies to any situation where enough infractions can tip a norm into a new, harder-to-reverse state
It guarantees that bad behavior will eventually self-correct without intervention
cards.correct_answer: It applies to any situation where enough infractions can tip a norm into a new, harder-to-reverse state
cards.explanation: The text extends herd immunity to 'maintaining social, cultural, business, and industry norms,' using Italy's 'Only fools pay' tax-evasion norm and litter-dirtied spaces as examples of a threshold being crossed into a hard-to-reverse state. The self-correction and genetic options contradict the text.

cards.question: The Coase theorem says an externality can be internalized efficiently without further intervention if which conditions are met?
options: Full government regulation, high taxes, and subsidies
Universal consent, equal wealth, and no externalities
Perfect competition, perfect information, and a single buyer
Well-defined property rights, rational actors, and low transaction costs
cards.correct_answer: Well-defined property rights, rational actors, and low transaction costs
cards.explanation: The text lists these three Coase conditions explicitly and uses cap-and-trade as a modern application: permits define rights, companies act rationally, and the open market keeps transaction costs low. The other options either invert the theorem or describe unrelated economic idealizations.

cards.question: In the text, what does 'internalizing' a negative externality mean?
options: Accepting the harm as an unavoidable cost of doing business
Shifting the externality onto a different affected party
Letting the market self-correct through competition
Requiring the entity that causes the externality to pay for it, ideally at a price high enough to cover the harm
cards.correct_answer: Requiring the entity that causes the externality to pay for it, ideally at a price high enough to cover the harm
cards.explanation: The text defines internalizing as making the causing entity pay — with cigarette taxes, traffic tolls, fines, regulation, and lawsuits as instruments, and a littering fine example showing that a high-enough price can also prevent the harm outright. The market-self-correcting and cost-shifting options aren't how the text frames it.

cards.question: 'Moral hazard' describes the situation where...
options: A person deliberately breaks a moral code to gain financial advantage
Insurance rates rise whenever more people file claims
People take on more risk once they believe they are more protected from its consequences
Agents are forced to take on their clients' risks as a condition of working
cards.correct_answer: People take on more risk once they believe they are more protected from its consequences
cards.explanation: The text opens with the rental-insurance example — drivers drive more recklessly after buying extra coverage — and defines moral hazard as taking on more risk 'once you have information that encourages you to believe you are more protected.' Deliberate immorality and forced risk transfer are not what the model names.

cards.question: Why, according to the Freakonomics analysis quoted in the text, do real estate agents often leave their own houses on the market longer than their clients'?
options: Agents have more patience than their clients
Agents legally cannot sell their homes during active client listings
Selling your own house faster triggers higher capital gains taxes
The agent's 1.5 percent commission on a small price gain is too tiny an incentive compared with the client's full gain
cards.correct_answer: The agent's 1.5 percent commission on a small price gain is too tiny an incentive compared with the client's full gain
cards.explanation: Levitt and Dubner's quoted analysis shows the agent's share of an extra $10,000 is $150, too puny to justify holding out — so for clients they push for quick deals, while for their own homes they wait ten days longer and earn 3+ percent more. The tax, patience, and legal options don't appear in the text.

cards.question: In the used-car 'market for lemons,' what sets off the death-spiral dynamic?
options: Buyers cannot distinguish good cars from bad ones, so owners of good cars withdraw them and average quality drops further
Sellers overprice good cars so buyers default to cheap bad ones
Regulators cap prices, forcing peaches out of the market
Buyers collude to drive prices below what sellers will accept
cards.correct_answer: Buyers cannot distinguish good cars from bad ones, so owners of good cars withdraw them and average quality drops further
cards.explanation: The text says buyers will only pay an average price; owners of peaches know their cars are worth more and pull them, so the average quality and price keep dropping until only lemons remain. The cap, collusion, and overpricing options are not the mechanism described.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: Goodhart's law warns that...
options: Any measurement contains random error
Measures with more decimal places are more reliable targets
Better measures always yield better targets
When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
cards.correct_answer: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
cards.explanation: The text states this phrasing (due to Strathern) explicitly, with the Soviet Gosplan nail-factory cartoon as a concrete illustration: weight-based targets produced one gigantic nail. The other options misrepresent the law's content.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: The 'cobra effect' names the failure mode where...
options: An attempted solution actually makes the underlying problem worse
Small infractions escalate into larger crimes over time
Competitors retaliate against punitive policies
Regulators deliberately mimic criminal tactics
cards.correct_answer: An attempted solution actually makes the underlying problem worse
cards.explanation: The text says the model describes 'when an attempted solution actually makes the problem worse,' grounding it in the British bounty for cobras in India, which ended up increasing the cobra population after breeders released their stocks. The other options don't describe the cobra effect.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: According to the text, what is the Streisand effect?
options: Celebrities attracting disproportionate media coverage
Legal threats succeeding at suppressing information online
Unintentionally drawing more attention to something by trying to hide it
Public figures benefiting financially from controversies
cards.correct_answer: Unintentionally drawing more attention to something by trying to hide it
cards.explanation: The text names the effect after Barbra Streisand's 2003 lawsuit, which took the mansion photo from six downloads to hundreds of thousands of views after news coverage. The suppression-succeeds option is the opposite of what the effect describes; the others are not what it names.

cards.question: What does the software-industry term 'technical debt' describe?
options: Money borrowed by software companies to fund engineering hires
Patent liabilities inherited when a company is acquired
The depreciation of software licenses over time
The consequences of prioritizing short-term code fixes over long-term, well-designed code
cards.correct_answer: The consequences of prioritizing short-term code fixes over long-term, well-designed code
cards.explanation: The text defines technical debt as the short-termism outcome in software: hacks accumulate and 'eventually have to be paid down by future code rewrites and refactors.' The borrowing, depreciation, and patent options are unrelated to the model.

cards.question: Why does the text recommend 'preserving optionality'?
options: To guarantee the highest single outcome available today
To lower costs by refusing to pay for alternatives
To let every choice ripen into a firm commitment by itself
To keep future options open so you can adapt as circumstances change or more information arrives
cards.correct_answer: To keep future options open so you can adapt as circumstances change or more information arrives
cards.explanation: The text describes preserving optionality as making choices that keep future options open — rainy-day funds, picking a college strong in several majors, delaying decisions to wait for more information. The guarantee-best-outcome and cost-lowering options contradict the text, which notes optionality usually costs more.

cards.question: The 'precautionary principle' advises that when an action could cause harm of unknown magnitude, you should...
options: Proceed immediately so you don't miss the opportunity
Delegate the decision to the affected parties only
Ignore the risk until empirical evidence of harm appears
Proceed with extreme caution before enacting the policy
cards.correct_answer: Proceed with extreme caution before enacting the policy
cards.explanation: The text states this directly and cites the EU 2012 Treaty adoption and the 'First, do no harm' medical analogy, plus examples like controlling suspected carcinogens tightly while science catches up. The proceed-immediately and ignore options invert the principle.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: In Jeff Bezos's 'one-way doors vs. two-way doors' framing, organizations most often err by...
options: Treating irreversible decisions as if they were reversible
Outsourcing all reversible decisions to junior staff
Ignoring reversibility entirely and deciding at random
Treating reversible decisions with the heavy-weight process meant for irreversible ones, causing slowness and risk aversion
cards.correct_answer: Treating reversible decisions with the heavy-weight process meant for irreversible ones, causing slowness and risk aversion
cards.explanation: Bezos's quoted letter says larger organizations tend 'to use the heavy-weight [irreversible] decision-making process on most decisions, including many [reversible] decisions,' producing 'slowness, unthoughtful risk aversion, failure to experiment sufficiently.' The other options are not claims the text makes.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: Hick's law, as used in the text, predicts that...
options: Decision accuracy falls to zero once choices exceed seven
People always pick the last option they consider
Decision time is independent of how many choices are available
Decision time grows logarithmically with the number of choices presented
cards.correct_answer: Decision time grows logarithmically with the number of choices presented
cards.explanation: The text says Hick and Hyman quantified 'the mathematical relationship between the number of choices given and how long it takes to decide' and found a logarithmic increase — applied to menus, navigation, and forms. The other options misrepresent the law.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: What does the parole-board study illustrate about decision fatigue?
options: Favorable rulings rose steadily throughout each session as judges warmed up
Prisoners' case quality, not fatigue, explained the ruling variation
Favorable rulings were unaffected by breaks or time of day
Favorable rulings dropped gradually from about 65% to near zero within a session and rebounded after a break
cards.correct_answer: Favorable rulings dropped gradually from about 65% to near zero within a session and rebounded after a break
cards.explanation: The text quotes the 2011 study directly: favorable rulings 'drops gradually from [about] 65% to nearly zero within each decision session and returns abruptly to [about] 65% after a break.' This grounds decision fatigue: quality worsens with accumulated decisions, resets after rest.

cards.section: You Can Do Anything, but Not Everything
cards.question: According to Paul Graham's 'top idea in your mind' essay as quoted in the text, what is dangerous about letting the wrong idea dominate your mental idle time?
options: You lose sleep worrying about it
Your peers will judge you for thinking about the wrong things
Your memory for unrelated facts decays more quickly
You cannot control this drift directly, and your unconscious creative thinking goes to that idea while others are starved of it
cards.correct_answer: You cannot control this drift directly, and your unconscious creative thinking goes to that idea while others are starved of it
cards.explanation: Graham's quoted passage says creative insight often comes while you're doing something else, that people have one 'top idea' their mind drifts toward, that you can only control it indirectly, and 'it's a disaster to let the wrong idea become the top one in your mind.' The sleep, memory, and peer-judgment options aren't the reason given.

cards.section: You Can Do Anything, but Not Everything
cards.question: The text cites 'Parkinson's law of triviality' (bike-shedding) to warn that organizations tend to...
options: Spend disproportionate time on trivial issues because they are easy and familiar
Delegate trivial decisions to the lowest-ranking member
Spend proportional time on each agenda item regardless of importance
Refuse to discuss major decisions without unanimous consent
cards.correct_answer: Spend disproportionate time on trivial issues because they are easy and familiar
cards.explanation: Parkinson's atomic-reactor-and-bike-shed committee example grounds the law: members dodge the hard reactor debate but everyone has opinions on the easy bike shed, so 'time spent on any item of the agenda will be in inverse proportion to the sum involved.' The proportional-time option is the exact opposite.

cards.section: You Can Do Anything, but Not Everything
cards.question: In negotiation, what does BATNA stand for in the book's usage?
options: Benchmark Against The Negotiating Average
Broad Array of Tactical Negotiation Aims
Binding Agreement Terms for Negotiated Assets
Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
cards.correct_answer: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
cards.explanation: The text spells out BATNA as 'best alternative to a negotiated agreement' and frames it as an application of opportunity cost: in a job negotiation, your BATNA is your best competing offer, and you shouldn't accept anything worse. The other options are invented expansions.

cards.section: Getting More Bang for Your Buck
cards.question: In the book's usage, the Pareto principle says roughly that...
options: Outcomes are always divided evenly across efforts
Approximately 80 percent of results come from about 20 percent of the effort
50 percent of effort produces 50 percent of results
The last 20 percent of effort produces 80 percent of results
cards.correct_answer: Approximately 80 percent of results come from about 20 percent of the effort
cards.explanation: The text states the principle explicitly and grounds it in Pareto's pea-pod and Italian land-ownership observations, plus modern examples like U.S. healthcare spending. Juran called the outsized-impact subset 'the vital few.' The even-distribution and inverted options contradict the principle.

cards.section: Getting More Bang for Your Buck
cards.question: The 'law of diminishing returns' tells you that...
options: Each additional hour of effort yields the same marginal impact as the previous one
Effort becomes more effective as you continue past a certain level
Continued effort tends to diminish in effectiveness after a certain level of result has been achieved
Early effort always produces no results until a threshold is crossed
cards.correct_answer: Continued effort tends to diminish in effectiveness after a certain level of result has been achieved
cards.explanation: The text defines it directly and uses Lauren's clinical-study-report example (drafts three through six added little) to ground it. The other options either describe constant returns, inverse returns, or ignore the initial productive phase.

cards.section: Getting More Bang for Your Buck
cards.question: When should you move on from an activity that has hit diminishing returns, according to the text?
options: Always, immediately
Never, as long as any marginal progress is happening
When you can identify another activity that produces greater results for the same effort
Only after you have hit clearly negative returns
cards.correct_answer: When you can identify another activity that produces greater results for the same effort
cards.explanation: The text says 'it really comes down to opportunity cost' — you should quit before hitting negative returns, but diminishing returns alone aren't reason to quit; only switch when another activity has higher leverage. The other options misstate this criterion.

cards.section: Get Out of Your Own Way
cards.question: Kahneman and Tversky's work on loss aversion, as cited in the text, found that people typically want the potential payoff in a fifty-fifty gamble to be about...
options: Equal to the potential loss
Half the potential loss
Double the potential loss
Five times the potential loss
cards.correct_answer: Double the potential loss
cards.explanation: The text cites their 1992 Journal of Risk and Uncertainty work: across risky situations like coin tosses, 'people tend to want the potential payoff to be around double the potential loss' — a fifty-fifty chance at $100 to risk $50. The other ratios are not what the study found.

cards.section: Get Out of Your Own Way
cards.question: The 'sunk-cost fallacy,' as the text describes it, happens when...
options: Previous irrecoverable costs cloud your decision making and lead you to keep investing in a failing path
You stop a project too early without accounting for its real value
You average future cash flows without discounting them
You confuse the price you paid with the market's current valuation
cards.correct_answer: Previous irrecoverable costs cloud your decision making and lead you to keep investing in a failing path
cards.explanation: The text defines sunk costs as 'irrecoverable costs' and warns the fallacy arises when 'these previous losses influence you to make a bad decision,' with the Concorde development program as a ready example. The stop-too-early and averaging options describe different issues.

cards.section: Get Out of Your Own Way
cards.question: What is a 'pre-mortem,' as defined in this section?
options: An autopsy performed before death is declared
A review after a project fails to find root causes
A technique where you predict ahead of time where things could go off track
A financial analysis run before investment
cards.correct_answer: A technique where you predict ahead of time where things could go off track
cards.explanation: The text explicitly introduces pre-mortems alongside mid-mortems, contrasting them with the end-of-project post-mortem: you 'predict ahead of time where things could go off track.' The autopsy and financial-analysis options are not what the text names.

cards.question: In the book's usage, a 'design pattern' is...
options: A set of colors chosen for a brand identity
A reusable solution to a design problem that has proven to work
A statistical pattern found in customer data
A marketing template for sales copy
cards.correct_answer: A reusable solution to a design problem that has proven to work
cards.explanation: The text credits architect Christopher Alexander with the concept and defines it as 'a reusable solution to a design problem,' giving doorknob heights and staircase widths as physical examples. The branding, statistical, and marketing options are not what the concept names.

cards.question: What is an 'anti-pattern' according to the text?
options: A pattern explicitly forbidden by industry standards
A seemingly intuitive but actually ineffective 'solution' to a common problem that often already has a better known solution
A security pattern designed to fool attackers
A pattern deliberately chosen for visual contrast
cards.correct_answer: A seemingly intuitive but actually ineffective 'solution' to a common problem that often already has a better known solution
cards.explanation: The text defines anti-patterns as the opposite of design patterns using exactly this phrasing, and lists bike-shedding, present bias, and negative returns as examples. The forbidden-pattern, security, and contrast options are not in the text.

cards.question: How does the text distinguish 'heuristic solutions' from 'brute force' approaches in password cracking?
options: Heuristics try every combination; brute force uses smart rules
Brute force tries every combination exhaustively, while heuristics use trial-and-error rules (like dictionary words) that aren't guaranteed optimal but are often effective
Heuristics are always faster and always correct
Brute force uses networks, heuristics use single computers
cards.correct_answer: Brute force tries every combination exhaustively, while heuristics use trial-and-error rules (like dictionary words) that aren't guaranteed optimal but are often effective
cards.explanation: The text says brute force is exhaustive search — 218 trillion combinations for an 8-char password — and heuristics are 'a trial-and-error solution that is not guaranteed to yield optimal or perfect results, but in many cases is nevertheless very effective,' with dictionary-word guessing as the canonical example. The other options invert or misrepresent this.

cards.question: According to the book, why is 'Culture eats strategy for breakfast' a useful warning?
options: Culture shifts faster than strategy, rendering plans obsolete
Strategy and culture must always be identical for an organization to function
Culture has more inertia than strategy, so a strategy in opposition to culture is very unlikely to succeed
Strategy is always more important than culture
cards.correct_answer: Culture has more inertia than strategy, so a strategy in opposition to culture is very unlikely to succeed
cards.explanation: The text explicitly attributes the warning to the fact that culture 'has much more inertia than even its strategy,' using the 2013 HealthCare.gov launch as a real-world mismatch. The other options either contradict the text or reverse its claim.

cards.question: What does the 'flywheel' mental model capture about sustained progress?
options: Once stopped, a flywheel is impossible to restart
Progress requires constant acceleration to matter
Great transformations happen in one fell swoop, not incrementally
It takes a lot of effort to start spinning, but once it is spinning, it takes little effort to keep it spinning
cards.correct_answer: It takes a lot of effort to start spinning, but once it is spinning, it takes little effort to keep it spinning
cards.explanation: The text uses the merry-go-round analogy and Jim Collins's Good to Great passage — 'no single defining action, no grand program . . . a cumulative process . . . turn by turn of the flywheel.' The one-fell-swoop option is exactly what the model denies.

cards.question: In the book's usage, a 'forcing function' is...
options: A prescheduled event or process that facilitates a desired action, like a standing meeting
A regulation imposed by government on private actors
A mathematical function that always has a solution
A threat that forces opponents to negotiate
cards.correct_answer: A prescheduled event or process that facilitates a desired action, like a standing meeting
cards.explanation: The text defines a forcing function as 'a prescheduled event, or function, that facilitates, or forces, you to take a desired action' and gives standing one-on-ones and weekly project updates as examples. The regulation, math, and threat options aren't how the text uses the term.

cards.question: Metcalfe's law, as described in the text, says that the value of a network grows...
options: Proportionally to the square of the number of connected nodes
Linearly with the number of nodes
Exponentially with the number of edges, regardless of nodes
Only once a fixed critical mass of nodes is reached
cards.correct_answer: Proportionally to the square of the number of connected nodes
cards.explanation: The text says connections grow 'proportionally to the square of the number of phones (nodes)' — 2 phones, 1 connection; 5 phones, 10; 12 phones, 66 — and names this relationship Metcalfe's law. The linear and exponential-in-edges options don't match the math shown; the critical-mass option describes a different concept.

cards.question: According to Everett Rogers's technology adoption life cycle as presented, which group makes up the smallest percentage of the population?
options: Innovators (about 2.5 percent)
Early adopters (13.5 percent)
Early majority (34 percent)
Laggards (16 percent)
cards.correct_answer: Innovators (about 2.5 percent)
cards.explanation: The text lists Rogers's five groups with exact percentages: innovators 2.5%, early adopters 13.5%, early/late majority 34% each, laggards 16%. Innovators are the smallest group.

cards.question: A 'cascading failure,' as described in the text, is...
options: A planned sequence of shutdowns during maintenance
A failure in one piece of a system that triggers a chain reaction of failure through the entire system
A failure limited to a single isolated component
A failure prevented by having many backup systems
cards.correct_answer: A failure in one piece of a system that triggers a chain reaction of failure through the entire system
cards.explanation: The text defines it that way and cites electrical-grid blackouts, the 2007/2008 financial crisis (subprime mortgages cascading through banks), and ecological food-chain collapse as examples. The planned, isolated, and prevented options don't match the definition.

cards.question: What is 'luck surface area' as the text describes it?
options: The amount of good fortune you are born with
The measurable area where random chance is known to operate
The insurance coverage needed to protect against bad luck
How much you increase your chances of beneficial encounters by interacting with more people in more diverse situations
cards.correct_answer: How much you increase your chances of beneficial encounters by interacting with more people in more diverse situations
cards.explanation: The text credits Jason Roberts with the term and uses the wide-net fishing metaphor: relaxing your rules for engagement and attending more events increases your luck surface area. The innate-luck and insurance options don't match; the measurable-area option misreads the metaphor.

cards.question: Why does the text call zero-sum thinking the exception rather than the rule in negotiations?
options: Because most negotiations involve multiple terms the parties value differently, enabling win-win trades
Because most negotiations are governed by strict regulation
Because most parties are willing to take losses for social reasons
Because the underlying math of negotiations rules out losses
cards.correct_answer: Because most negotiations involve multiple terms the parties value differently, enabling win-win trades
cards.explanation: The text says most negotiations 'don't include just one term, such as price, but instead involve many terms,' so each party can give things they value less for things they value more — producing win-win outcomes. Regulation, social altruism, and loss-proof math are not what the text cites.

cards.question: How does the 'second law of thermodynamics' apply as a metaphor in the book?
options: Relationships and orderly spaces naturally improve without attention
Orderliness must be actively maintained because, in a closed system, entropy naturally increases over time
Closed systems eventually reach a stable, ordered equilibrium
Heat only flows from cold to hot objects in daily life
cards.correct_answer: Orderliness must be actively maintained because, in a closed system, entropy naturally increases over time
cards.explanation: The text says the law 'serves as a reminder that orderliness needs to be maintained, lest it be slowly chipped away by disorder' — broken eggs don't mend, shaken puzzles don't reassemble, relationships decay without effort. The auto-improvement and ordered-equilibrium options are the opposite of what the law says.

cards.question: What does the text identify as the main problem with drawing conclusions from anecdotal evidence?
options: Anecdotes are often not representative of the full range of experiences, because people disproportionately share out-of-the-ordinary stories
Anecdotes take too long to collect
Anecdotes are always deliberately fabricated
Anecdotes have no relationship to reality at all
cards.correct_answer: Anecdotes are often not representative of the full range of experiences, because people disproportionately share out-of-the-ordinary stories
cards.explanation: The text says 'people are more inclined to share out-of-the-ordinary stories' — e.g., only terrible or amazing reviews — so anecdotes aren't representative. The other options either overclaim fabrication or exaggerate unusability.

cards.question: What is a 'placebo' in the fake-surgery studies the text describes?
options: A drug that is known to be active but administered at a low dose
Something given to control participants that looks and feels like the real treatment but is supposed to have no effect
A surgical instrument used only in training
A side effect intentionally induced in the experimental group
cards.correct_answer: Something given to control participants that looks and feels like the real treatment but is supposed to have no effect
cards.explanation: The text defines a placebo as something that 'looks and feels like what the experimental participants receive, but in reality is supposed to have no effect,' with sham surgeries (scope inserted, nothing done) as the vivid example. The low-dose and side-effect options don't match the definition.

cards.question: Why do researchers blind experiments where possible, according to the text?
options: To speed up data collection
To prevent participants' conscious and unconscious biases from influencing results, and further to reduce observer-expectancy bias
To legally protect participants' identities
To guarantee a positive result
cards.correct_answer: To prevent participants' conscious and unconscious biases from influencing results, and further to reduce observer-expectancy bias
cards.explanation: The text says blinded experiments keep participants unaware of their group 'preventing their conscious and unconscious bias from influencing the results,' and double-blinding adds protection against researcher observer-expectancy bias. The speed, legal, and guaranteed-positive options aren't the reason given.

cards.question: 'Nonresponse bias' arises when...
options: A survey has an overly complex question structure
Participants with a relevant connection to the survey topic systematically fail to respond, biasing the sample
Respondents answer randomly instead of truthfully
Researchers discard inconvenient responses from the data
cards.correct_answer: Participants with a relevant connection to the survey topic systematically fail to respond, biasing the sample
cards.explanation: The text uses the employee engagement example: disengaged employees skip the survey due to apathy — a nonrandom reason tied to the survey topic — so their views are missed. Random missing (scheduled vacation) doesn't bias results. The other options describe different issues (response bias, fraud).

cards.question: According to the text, why might dropout-founder stories (Gates, Zuckerberg) mislead you about dropping out?
options: Because surviving dropouts are over-represented while the many failed dropouts are invisible — survivorship bias
Because founders often exaggerate their success
Because dropouts face legal penalties that aren't widely reported
Because credentials are becoming less valuable generally
cards.correct_answer: Because surviving dropouts are over-represented while the many failed dropouts are invisible — survivorship bias
cards.explanation: The text uses exactly this example: you're 'thinking only of the people that survived. You're missing all the dropouts who did not make it to the top.' The exaggeration, legal-penalty, and credential-devaluation options aren't what the text cites.

cards.section: Be Wary of the “Law” of Small Numbers
cards.question: The 'clustering illusion' describes the tendency to...
options: Erroneously interpret naturally occurring streaks and clusters in random data as evidence of non-random behavior
Correctly identify that clusters reveal underlying structure
Always underestimate how common streaks are in random sequences
Assume only clusters, never individual events, matter
cards.correct_answer: Erroneously interpret naturally occurring streaks and clusters in random data as evidence of non-random behavior
cards.explanation: The text defines the clustering illusion as 'a failure of intuition' and gives the WWII London bombing conspiracy theories as an example: statistical analysis showed the bombings were random, but people read patterns into clusters. The other options either reverse the error or misrepresent it.

cards.section: Be Wary of the “Law” of Small Numbers
cards.question: 'Regression to the mean' explains which of the following?
options: A rookie's strong first season is most likely followed by caving under pressure
Extreme results are usually followed by something more typical, because repeats of rare events are themselves rare
Averages always trend upward over time
Small samples converge faster to the truth than large samples
cards.correct_answer: Extreme results are usually followed by something more typical, because repeats of rare events are themselves rare
cards.explanation: The text says extreme events tend to be followed by 'something more typical, regressing closer to the expected mean' — and explicitly explains the sophomore slump this way rather than by psychology. The small-sample-convergence option is the opposite of the law of large numbers.

cards.question: In a normal distribution, approximately what percentage of values fall within one standard deviation of the mean?
options: About 50 percent
About 68 percent
About 95 percent
About 99.7 percent
cards.correct_answer: About 68 percent
cards.explanation: The text gives these figures directly: about 68% within one standard deviation, about 95% within two, and nearly all (99.7%) within three. The 50% and 99.7% options belong to other bands or other distributions entirely.

cards.question: The central limit theorem, as used in this section, says that...
options: All real-world data is normally distributed
