cards.question: An infant who cannot be vaccinated benefits when others vaccinate but is harmed when vaccination rates drop. These outcomes are best described as:
options: Market failures — the vaccination market is failing to protect the infant
Positive and negative externalities — spillover effects from others' decisions affect the infant without its consent
Free rider consequences — the infant is a free rider on others' vaccinations
Coase theorem outcomes — the infant's rights to a disease-free environment are unpriced
cards.correct_answer: Positive and negative externalities — spillover effects from others' decisions affect the infant without its consent
cards.explanation: The text explicitly uses the infant vaccination example to illustrate externalities: 'The infant receives a positive externality from those who choose to vaccinate and a negative externality from those who do not.' These arise from spillover effects imposed from an external source without the infant's consent.

cards.question: Buying a car adds congestion to shared roads, costing other drivers time. This is an example of which concept?
options: Moral hazard — you drive more because you have insurance
Spillover effect — an effect of your action spills over to others who share the roads
Tragedy of the commons — every car owner is equally depleting the road commons
Adverse selection — people who drive more choose to buy cars
cards.correct_answer: Spillover effect — an effect of your action spills over to others who share the roads
cards.explanation: The text uses car congestion as the direct example for spillover effects: 'when you buy a car, you add congestion to the roads you drive on, a cost borne by everyone.' Spillover effects are the mechanism by which externalities occur — the cost of your activity spills over outside the core interaction.

cards.question: Cigarette taxes and higher health insurance premiums for smokers are best understood as:
options: Adverse selection mechanisms that filter out high-risk individuals from the insurance pool
Ways of internalizing the negative externalities of smoking by requiring smokers to bear more of the costs
Coase theorem applications that create a marketplace for smoking rights
Goodhart's law violations because smoking rates become a government target
cards.correct_answer: Ways of internalizing the negative externalities of smoking by requiring smokers to bear more of the costs
cards.explanation: The text explicitly lists cigarette taxes and smoker insurance premiums as examples of internalizing externalities: making the actor pay for the cost they impose on others. Internalization is the goal; the Coase theorem involves a private marketplace, not government taxes.

cards.question: The Coase theorem holds that externalities can be internalized efficiently without government intervention when which conditions are met?
options: When the parties involved have similar levels of power and income
When well-defined property rights, rational actors, and low transaction costs exist
When the externality affects a large enough population to make legal action cost-effective
When the market has been operating long enough to establish equilibrium prices
cards.correct_answer: When well-defined property rights, rational actors, and low transaction costs exist
cards.explanation: The text lists the Coase theorem's three conditions exactly: '1. Well-defined property rights. 2. Rational actors. 3. Low transaction costs.' Cap-and-trade systems are given as a real-world example of applying these conditions. Power equality and market maturity are not among Coase's conditions.

cards.question: Studies show people drive rental cars more recklessly after purchasing extra rental insurance. This is moral hazard because:
options: The insurance company has more information about the renter's driving habits than the renter does
The renter takes on more risk because they believe they are financially protected from consequences
The renter is free-riding on the insurance pool funded by careful drivers
The insurance company created a perverse incentive by pricing coverage the same for all drivers
cards.correct_answer: The renter takes on more risk because they believe they are financially protected from consequences
cards.explanation: The text defines moral hazard as taking on more 'hazard' once you have information that you are more protected. Protection from financial consequences changes behavior toward greater risk-taking. Free-riding applies to public goods; the mechanism here is the belief in protection reducing the personal cost of risky behavior.

cards.question: A real estate agent sells your home quickly at the first decent offer rather than waiting for the best price. This is primarily a:
options: Adverse selection — the agent selected clients based on private information about market conditions
Asymmetric information problem — the agent withheld knowledge about the market from you
Principal-agent problem — the agent's self-interest (fast commission) conflicts with your interest (best price)
Moral hazard — the agent takes risks with your property because they don't bear the full consequences
cards.correct_answer: Principal-agent problem — the agent's self-interest (fast commission) conflicts with your interest (best price)
cards.explanation: The text uses the Freakonomics real estate agent example to illustrate the principal-agent problem: the agent earns only $150 more from an extra $10,000 on your sale, so they favor speed over the best price for you. Asymmetric information is the underlying enabler, but the named problem is the agent's self-interest overriding the principal's.

cards.question: Disclosure laws requiring financial advisers to reveal their compensation when recommending products are designed to combat which problem?
options: Moral hazard — advisers take excessive risks when their upside is guaranteed
Adverse selection — high-risk clients are more likely to seek financial advice
Asymmetric information — clients lack information about how adviser compensation affects recommendations
Principal-agent problem — making advisers legally liable for recommendations aligns their incentives
cards.correct_answer: Asymmetric information — clients lack information about how adviser compensation affects recommendations
cards.explanation: The text explains that advisers' compensation structures are 'not always completely transparent to principals,' and 'disclosure laws... can reduce the effects of asymmetric information.' The laws fix the information gap. Moral hazard is about risk-taking behavior, not information gaps.

cards.question: In the used car market, why do sellers of good cars ('peaches') withdraw from the market when buyers can't distinguish them from bad cars ('lemons')?
options: Because peach sellers don't want to deal with the transaction costs of the market
Because peach sellers know their cars are worth more than the average price buyers are willing to pay
Because peach sellers face moral hazard from buyers who might mistreat quality cars
Because peach sellers experience a Streisand effect when they advertise their cars' quality
cards.correct_answer: Because peach sellers know their cars are worth more than the average price buyers are willing to pay
cards.explanation: The text explains: 'Sellers who know their cars are peaches will not want to sell them in this market because they know their cars are worth more than the average price.' This is adverse selection in action — information asymmetry causes self-selection of worse-quality goods into the market, eventually collapsing its average quality.

cards.question: Which of the following is the best definition of 'market failure' as used in the chapter?
options: A market crash caused by excessive speculation and leverage
A situation where open markets without intervention create suboptimal results
A market that collapses because of insufficient consumer demand
A regulatory regime that prevents markets from setting competitive prices
cards.correct_answer: A situation where open markets without intervention create suboptimal results
cards.explanation: The text gives an explicit definition: 'market failure, where open markets without intervention can create suboptimal results, or fail.' It then lists tragedy of the commons, externalities, moral hazard, and information asymmetry as contributors. Market crashes are a different concept entirely.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: Soviet factories rewarded for nail count made tiny pinlike nails by the millions; rewarded by weight, they made one enormous nail. What principle does this illustrate?
options: Cobra effect — the reward system made the problem (nail scarcity) worse
Goodhart's law — when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
Tyranny of small decisions — individual factory decisions aggregated into a systemic problem
Adverse selection — factories with poor-quality nails self-selected into the reward system
cards.correct_answer: Goodhart's law — when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
cards.explanation: The text uses the Soviet nail production example to open the Goodhart's law discussion: targeting the metric (nail count or weight) causes behavior that optimizes the metric but not the underlying goal. The cobra effect describes when a solution makes the original problem worse — a related but distinct pattern.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: How does Campbell's law extend Goodhart's law?
options: Campbell's law applies only to social indicators, while Goodhart's law applies to economic ones
Campbell's law adds that quantitative social indicators used for decisions become subject to corruption and distort the very processes they measure
Campbell's law holds that the measure improves when the target is made more specific
Campbell's law predicts the exact time frame in which a measure will become corrupted
cards.correct_answer: Campbell's law adds that quantitative social indicators used for decisions become subject to corruption and distort the very processes they measure
cards.explanation: The text says Campbell's law 'explains the concept a bit more precisely': quantitative indicators used for decision-making become 'subject to corruption pressures' and distort the social processes they monitor. Both laws describe the same basic phenomenon; Campbell's adds the specific mechanism of corruption and distortion.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: The British in India offered cobra bounties to reduce snake populations. Entrepreneurs started breeding cobras; when the program ended, they released all of them. This demonstrates:
options: Goodhart's law — the cobra count became a target that ceased to be a good measure
The cobra effect — an attempted solution actually made the original problem worse
The hydra effect — cutting the cobra population caused it to grow multiple new heads
Murphy's law — anything that can go wrong, will go wrong
cards.correct_answer: The cobra effect — an attempted solution actually made the original problem worse
cards.explanation: The text defines the cobra effect through this exact story and names it accordingly. While Goodhart's law is the upstream cause (incentivizing dead cobras distorted behavior), the cobra effect specifically names the pattern where the solution worsens the original problem — ending with a larger cobra population than before.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: Barbra Streisand sued to keep an aerial photo of her home private. Before the suit, the photo had been downloaded six times. What happened after the lawsuit became news?
options: The photo was permanently removed from the internet due to the legal proceedings
The site was visited hundreds of thousands of times, and the photo became widely available
Public sympathy for the photographer caused increased scrutiny of celebrity privacy laws
The lawsuit succeeded and set a precedent that reduced aerial photography
cards.correct_answer: The site was visited hundreds of thousands of times, and the photo became widely available
cards.explanation: The text describes this exact outcome: before the suit, six downloads; after news of the suit, hundreds of thousands of visits, and the photo is now freely licensed on Wikipedia. Attempting to suppress the image backfired by drawing massive attention — the defining Streisand effect.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: When authorities shut down one piracy website, multiple new ones appear to replace it. This is the hydra effect because:
options: The original site eventually returned after the legal pressure subsided
Suppressing one instance of the problem causes multiple new ones to grow in its place, like the mythological Hydra
The shutdown attracted public attention to the piracy issue, making it worse
The shutdown created perverse incentives for pirates to be more technically sophisticated
cards.correct_answer: Suppressing one instance of the problem causes multiple new ones to grow in its place, like the mythological Hydra
cards.explanation: The text defines the hydra effect with this movie piracy example: 'When you shut down an internet site where people share illegal movies or music, more pop up in its place.' The name comes from the Greek myth of the Hydra, which grew two heads for each one cut off. The Streisand effect is about drawing attention, not multiplication.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: Using a tire pressure gauge to measure pressure also releases some air, reducing the actual pressure. What does this illustrate?
options: The chilling effect — measuring the tire pressure discourages normal driving behavior
The cobra effect — a solution (measuring pressure) worsens the problem (low pressure)
The observer effect — the act of observation changes the thing being observed
Moral hazard — knowing the pressure makes drivers take on more road risk
cards.correct_answer: The observer effect — the act of observation changes the thing being observed
cards.explanation: The text uses the tire gauge as a direct example of the observer effect: measuring the tire pressure requires releasing air, which reduces the pressure. The act of measuring changes the measured quantity. The chilling effect concerns behavior change due to perceived surveillance, not an intrinsic measurement artifact.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: After Snowden's NSA revelations in 2013, Wikipedia traffic to terrorism-related articles dropped 20%. This is a chilling effect because:
options: The NSA directly blocked access to those Wikipedia articles
People stopped reading those articles out of fear that their browsing could attract government scrutiny
Fewer people were genuinely interested in terrorism topics after the revelations
Wikipedia improved its privacy settings, making those articles harder to find
cards.correct_answer: People stopped reading those articles out of fear that their browsing could attract government scrutiny
cards.explanation: The text cites the Penny study as an example of the chilling effect: people changed behavior (stopped reading) not because access was blocked, but because they felt observed and feared retaliation. This is a type of observer effect where the threat of surveillance changes behavior proactively.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: People who share names with individuals on the US No Fly List are sometimes denied boarding. This illustrates which mental model?
options: Adverse selection — the list disproportionately targets certain demographics
Blowback — the anti-terrorism policy harms the government's own citizens
Collateral damage — the policy's negative effects extend to unintended, innocent parties
The observer effect — being watched changes these travelers' behavior
cards.correct_answer: Collateral damage — the policy's negative effects extend to unintended, innocent parties
cards.explanation: The text uses No Fly List false positives as the example for collateral damage: 'negative side effects that result from an action,' harming unintended targets. Blowback is when the entity causing harm is itself harmed — the government isn't harmed the same way here, making collateral damage the precise term.

cards.section: Be Careful What You Wish For
cards.question: The US armed Afghan insurgents in the 1980s against the USSR. Years later, some of these groups used those same weapons against the US. This is a classic example of:
options: Hydra effect — suppressing the USSR created a new enemy from its remnants
Collateral damage — Afghans were unintended victims of US-Soviet geopolitics
Blowback — the harm the US created rebounded to affect the US itself
Cobra effect — the US attempt to weaken the USSR worsened its own security situation
cards.correct_answer: Blowback — the harm the US created rebounded to affect the US itself
cards.explanation: The text defines blowback as 'collateral damage that impacts the entity that inflicted it in the first place.' The US armed the insurgents; those same weapons were later used against the US — a classic case where the initiating actor suffers from its own action. Blowback is specifically when the actor bears the harm.

cards.question: The boiling frog metaphor is used in the chapter to caution against which pattern?
options: Acting too quickly on short-term signals without considering long-term implications
Failing to perceive or react to gradual, incremental change until it results in a catastrophic outcome
Overreacting to a single dramatic event that distorts your long-term planning
Ignoring expert warnings because they seem alarmist
cards.correct_answer: Failing to perceive or react to gradual, incremental change until it results in a catastrophic outcome
cards.explanation: The text describes the boiling frog as 'a useful mental model describing how a gradual change can be hard to react to, or even perceive.' Examples include climate change, abusive relationships, and mass surveillance — all situations where slow drift is hard to notice until severe harm has occurred.

cards.question: A company focuses intensely on quarterly earnings while neglecting five-year product development. What is the likely long-term consequence?
options: The company will become antifragile by streamlining to only what matters now
The company risks falling behind competitors who make long-term investments and may be disrupted by newcomers
The company will accumulate technical debt but remain competitive through agility
The company's short-term profits will eventually fund the long-term investments
cards.correct_answer: The company risks falling behind competitors who make long-term investments and may be disrupted by newcomers
cards.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'If you focus on just short-term financial results, you won't invest enough in the future. Eventually you will be left behind by competitors who are making those long-term investments, or you could be swiftly disrupted by new upstarts.' Short-termism is a direct path to competitive disadvantage.

cards.question: Technical debt in software development is the consequence of which type of short-term thinking?
options: Choosing open-source tools over proprietary ones to reduce licensing costs
Prioritizing quick code hacks over well-designed long-term systems, creating future rework obligations
Delaying user testing until after launch to save time during development
Releasing features before writing documentation
cards.correct_answer: Prioritizing quick code hacks over well-designed long-term systems, creating future rework obligations
cards.explanation: The text defines technical debt as what happens 'if you prioritize short-term code fixes, or hacks, over long-term, well-designed code and processes.' The debt must eventually be repaid through future code rewrites and refactors. Open-source choices and documentation delays are not the mechanism described.

cards.question: A startup adopts project management software casually. Years later, thousands of employees use it and all data is stored there, making switching feel impossible. This demonstrates:
options: Technical debt — the software choice was a short-term hack
Short-termism — they focused on short-term ease over long-term flexibility
Path dependence — an early, seemingly small decision now constrains available future choices
Analysis paralysis — the fear of choosing wrong software led to defaulting to the first option
cards.correct_answer: Path dependence — an early, seemingly small decision now constrains available future choices
cards.explanation: The text uses exactly this scenario as an example of path dependence: 'a set of decisions, or paths, available to you now is dependent on your past decisions.' The initial choice seemed innocuous but created lock-in. Technical debt specifically concerns code quality; path dependence is the broader economic concept about how early decisions foreclose future options.

cards.question: A college freshman who is unsure of their major enrolls in a school with strong programs across several fields of interest. What mental model are they applying?
options: Thinking gray — they are delaying a final opinion until they have more information
Precautionary principle — they are avoiding potential harm from a wrong major choice
Preserving optionality — they are keeping future choices open until they have more certainty
Ockham's razor — they chose the simplest college-selection criteria
cards.correct_answer: Preserving optionality — they are keeping future choices open until they have more certainty
cards.explanation: The text uses this exact college example for preserving optionality: 'choose a school that is strong in several fields of interest... which preserves their options until they are really ready to decide.' Thinking gray is about withholding judgment; preserving optionality is specifically about making choices today that keep future doors open.

cards.question: If a substance is suspected of causing cancer but evidence is not yet conclusive, the precautionary principle advises:
options: Wait for definitive scientific proof before restricting the substance to avoid regulatory overreach
Allow free-market pressure to drive companies to reduce the substance if demand decreases
Control the substance tightly now while research proceeds, rather than risk unnecessary harm
Apply Ockham's razor and assume no cancer risk until proven otherwise
cards.correct_answer: Control the substance tightly now while research proceeds, rather than risk unnecessary harm
cards.explanation: The text states: 'if there is reason to believe a substance might cause cancer, the precautionary principle advises that it is better to control it tightly now while the scientific community figures out the degree of harm.' It is specifically about acting carefully when harm is uncertain but potentially large — not waiting for proof.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: A person stays in a job they dislike for years because the sheer number of career options feels overwhelming. Which mental model best describes this?
options: Decision fatigue — they have made so many career decisions that quality has declined
Paradox of choice — too many options created anxiety, leading to inaction as the default
Analysis paralysis — overanalyzing the large number of available options causes decision-making to stall
Path dependence — their past career choices have constrained their future options
cards.correct_answer: Analysis paralysis — overanalyzing the large number of available options causes decision-making to stall
cards.explanation: The text uses exactly this example to illustrate analysis paralysis: 'people often stay in a job they don't like because they are unsure of what to do next given all the possibilities.' Analysis paralysis names the mechanism of being stuck due to over-analysis. Paradox of choice describes the resulting anxiety; analysis paralysis names the decision failure itself.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: 'If you wait for the perfect decision, you may be waiting a long time — and by not choosing, you are choosing the status quo.' What mental model does this illustrate?
options: Preserving optionality — delay keeps future paths open
Thinking gray — avoiding binary decisions maintains flexibility
Perfect is the enemy of good — insisting on perfection leads to inaction, which may be worse than an imperfect choice
Precautionary principle — caution before committing to any irreversible path
cards.correct_answer: Perfect is the enemy of good — insisting on perfection leads to inaction, which may be worse than an imperfect choice
cards.explanation: The text states this model directly: waiting for the perfect option means 'you are actually making a choice: you are choosing the status quo, which could be considerably worse than one of the other choices you could already have made.' Preserving optionality and thinking gray concern delaying for good reasons; this model warns that delay itself has costs.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: Jeff Bezos describes some decisions as 'two-way doors' and others as 'one-way doors.' What practical implication does this distinction have?
options: Two-way door decisions should always involve group consensus; one-way doors should be made by senior leadership alone
One-way door (irreversible) decisions need slow, deliberate processes; two-way door decisions should be made quickly and experimentally
Irreversible decisions should be made by AI systems to avoid cognitive bias
All major decisions should be treated as one-way doors to encourage proper deliberation
cards.correct_answer: One-way door (irreversible) decisions need slow, deliberate processes; two-way door decisions should be made quickly and experimentally
cards.explanation: The text quotes Bezos directly: one-way doors 'must be made methodically, carefully, slowly,' while two-way doors 'are changeable, reversible' and should not be slowed down. The danger Bezos warns against is applying the irreversible-decision process to reversible ones, which creates unnecessary slowness and risk aversion.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: A restaurant separates its menu into sections (pasta, pizza, salads) to help diners decide. How does this apply Hick's law?
options: It reduces cognitive dissonance by grouping similar items together
It applies anchoring by placing the most profitable items first
It reduces total decision time by creating a multi-step decision with fewer choices at each step
It eliminates the paradox of choice by offering fewer total dishes
cards.correct_answer: It reduces total decision time by creating a multi-step decision with fewer choices at each step
cards.explanation: The text describes Hick's law advice: 'give yourself or others a multi-step decision with fewer choices at each step.' Categories let diners first choose a food type, then choose within that category — consistent with Hick's finding that decision time grows logarithmically with number of simultaneous choices.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: Someone told there are 'plenty of fish in the sea' in dating often feels anxious rather than hopeful. According to the paradox of choice, why?
options: Because too many choices make it statistically impossible to find a good match
Because an abundance of options, the fear of a suboptimal choice, and potential regret over missed alternatives cause unhappiness
Because dating pools with more options have lower average partner quality
Because Hick's law makes decision time for choosing a partner logarithmically long
cards.correct_answer: Because an abundance of options, the fear of a suboptimal choice, and potential regret over missed alternatives cause unhappiness
cards.explanation: The text cites Schwartz's paradox of choice framework: 'an overabundance of choice, the fear of making a suboptimal decision, and the potential for lingering regret following missed opportunities can leave people unhappy.' The text uses the dating example directly. The problem is psychological anxiety from too many options, not statistical impossibility.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: A study of parole boards found that favorable rulings drop from ~65% to near zero within a session and reset to ~65% after a break. What does this demonstrate?
options: Availability bias — recent cases at the end of a session are judged more harshly
Anchoring — early rulings set a favorable standard that later rulings are judged against
Decision fatigue — quality of decisions deteriorates after a series of decisions, then recovers with rest
Analysis paralysis — the growing backlog of cases overwhelms the board's decision-making capacity
cards.correct_answer: Decision fatigue — quality of decisions deteriorates after a series of decisions, then recovers with rest
cards.explanation: The text cites this 2011 parole study directly as the illustration of decision fatigue: favorable rulings drop to nearly zero by the end of each session and jump back after a break. The pattern shows that decision quality — not just speed — is depleted by a series of decisions and restored by rest.

cards.section: Too Much of a Good Thing
cards.question: Murphy's law is described in the chapter as a defensive reminder. What behavior should it prompt?
options: Avoiding all high-stakes decisions that carry uncertainty
Expecting the worst outcome in every situation to maintain a pessimistic but realistic worldview
Anticipating that things will go wrong and having plans ready to respond when they do
Delegating all complex decisions to reduce the chance of personal error
cards.correct_answer: Anticipating that things will go wrong and having plans ready to respond when they do
cards.explanation: The text clarifies Murphy's original intent: it 'was intended as a defensive suggestion, to remind you to be prepared and to have a plan for when things go wrong.' It does not counsel avoiding decisions or assuming only the worst; it is a planning heuristic. Total pessimism without action is not the intended use.
